title: Determiner
definition: A determiner is a word placed before a noun to make its meaning clear — showing which one, whose, or how many/much.
rules: Articles are determiners: a, an, the.
Possessives: my, your, his, her, its, our, their.
Quantifiers: some, any, many, much, few, little, all, every.
Numbers can act as determiners: one, two, first, second.
examples: I have a pen and an eraser.
This shirt is nicer than that one.
Every student must submit the assignment.

commonMistakes.why: Use 'many' with countable nouns (friends) and 'much' with uncountable nouns (time, water).
'Information' is uncountable, so use 'this' (singular), not 'these', and no plural 's'.

commonMistakes.right: He is a friend of mine.
commonMistakes.why: Singular countable nouns need a determiner like 'a' before them.

quiz.question: Choose the correct determiner: 'I don't have ___ money.'
quiz.explanation: 'Much' is used with uncountable nouns like 'money'.

quiz.question: Choose the correct determiner: '___ students passed the exam.'
quiz.explanation: 'All' can be used with plural countable nouns like 'students'.

quiz.question: ___ books on the table are mine. (demonstrative, plural)
quiz.explanation: 'These' is the plural demonstrative for nearby objects.
